structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
# Description of PepsiCo Form 10-K Annual Report (Fiscal Year 2022)

## Main Points Covered:

**Document Type and Filing Information:**
- Annual Report (Form 10-K) for fiscal year ended December 31, 2022
- Filed with the SEC by PepsiCo, Inc., a North Carolina-incorporated company
- Company is a large accelerated filer and well-known seasoned issuer
- Common stock trades on Nasdaq under ticker symbol PEP

**Company Overview:**
- Leading global beverage and convenient food company
- Incorporated in Delaware in 1919, reincorporated in North Carolina in 1986
- Portfolio includes major brands: Lay's, Doritos, Cheetos, Gatorade, Pepsi-Cola, Mountain Dew, Quaker, and SodaStream
- Operates in more than 200 countries and territories

**Organizational Structure:**
- Seven reportable business segments including:
  - Frito-Lay North America (FLNA)
  - Quaker Foods North America (QFNA)
  - PepsiCo Beverages North America (PBNA)
  - Latin America (LatAm)
  - Europe
  - (Two additional segments referenced but not detailed in excerpt)

**Securities Information:**
- Multiple senior notes and bonds registered on Nasdaq with various maturity dates (2024-2050)
- Market capitalization of $224.2 billion as of June 10, 2022
- 1,377,251,316 shares outstanding as of February 2, 2023

**Report Contents:**
- Comprehensive coverage of business operations, risk factors, financial condition, management analysis, and corporate governance
- Forward-looking statements with associated risk disclosures

structure.title: Business
structure.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document is the opening section of PepsiCo's Annual Report on Form 10-K, covering the following main points:

## Forward-Looking Statements
- Identifies statements about future performance that constitute forward-looking statements under the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995
- Notes that such statements involve risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results to differ materially from predictions
- Directs investors to Item 1A (Risk Factors) and Item 7 (Management's Discussion and Analysis) for detailed risk information

## Company Overview and Business Operations
- PepsiCo is a leading global beverage and convenient food company incorporated in Delaware (1919) and reincorporated in North Carolina (1986)
- Operates in more than 200 countries and territories with major brands including Lay's, Doritos, Gatorade, Pepsi-Cola, Mountain Dew, Quaker, and SodaStream
- Organized into seven reportable segments: Frito-Lay North America, Quaker Foods North America, PepsiCo Beverages North America, Latin America, Europe, Africa/Middle East/South Asia, and Asia Pacific/Australia/New Zealand/China

## Distribution Network
- Uses multiple distribution channels: Direct-Store-Delivery (DSD), customer warehouse systems, distributor networks, and e-commerce platforms

## Supply Chain and Ingredients
- Details principal ingredients, packaging materials, and commodities used
- Notes 2022 challenges including increased commodity costs, supply constraints, and disruptions related to Ukraine conflict, COVID-19, inflation, and labor shortages

## Brands and Intellectual Property
- Owns numerous valuable trademarks essential to worldwide operations
- Holds patents related to products, packaging, and manufacturing processes

## Customers and Competition
- Major customers include distributors, retailers, and foodservice operators
- Walmart represents approximately 14% of consolidated net revenue
- Primary competitors include The Coca-Cola Company and other international and regional beverage/food companies
- Competes on brand recognition, taste, price, quality, innovation, and distribution

## Research and Development
- Invests in innovation globally with research centers worldwide
- Focuses on new product development, manufacturing improvements, sustainability, and digital technology

## Regulatory Matters
- Subject to extensive U.S. and international laws covering food safety, environmental protection, employment, competition, data privacy, and product labeling
- Faces increasing regulations on product taxes, labeling requirements, and packaging restrictions, particularly regarding sugar content and single-use plastics

## Human Capital
- Employed approximately 315,000 people worldwide as of December 31, 2022
- Emphasizes workplace safety, diversity and inclusion, and employee development
- Global workforce approximately 27% female; 44% of management roles female
- Completed over 1 million hours of employee training in 2022

## Available Information
- SEC filings and corporate information available on company website and SEC.gov

structure.title: Risk Factors
structure.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Initiatives
- Global workforce composition: 27% female overall, 44% female in management roles
- U.S. workforce diversity: 48% racially/ethnically diverse, 33% in managerial roles
- CEO's direct reports include 7 racially/ethnically diverse and/or female executives
- Employee training: Over 1 million hours of training completed in 2022

## Available Information and Investor Communications
- SEC filings available on SEC website and PepsiCo's corporate website
- Material information announced through SEC filings, press releases, conference calls, and webcasts
- Website information not incorporated into SEC filings

## Risk Factors - Business Risks
- Ukraine conflict impacts on operations, supply chain, and geopolitical uncertainty
- Demand reduction risks from changing consumer preferences and trends
- Reputation and brand image damage risks
- Product recall and quality/safety concerns
- Competition effectiveness challenges
- Workforce attraction, development, and retention difficulties
- Water scarcity impacts on manufacturing and supply chain
- Retail landscape changes and key customer dependency
- Manufacturing and supply chain disruptions with commodity/input cost volatility
- Political and social conditions affecting business
- Developing and emerging markets growth challenges
- Economic condition fluctuations
- Cybersecurity and information systems disruption risks
- Strategic transaction completion and management risks
- Third-party service provider reliance risks
- Climate change and environmental regulation impacts
- Labor strikes and work stoppages

## Risk Factors - Financial and Legal/Tax/Regulatory Risks
- Productivity initiatives implementation challenges
- Goodwill and asset impairment risks
- Foreign exchange rate fluctuations
- Credit rating downgrades affecting borrowing costs
- Product-specific taxes and marketing limitations
- Plastic and packaging material regulations
- Personal data protection and privacy law compliance
- Income tax rate and law changes
- Intellectual property protection challenges
- General regulatory compliance requirements
- Litigation and legal proceedings liabilities

structure.title: Unresolved Staff Comments
structure.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Compliance and Regulatory Risks
- Emphasis on compliance with competition, antitrust, and anti-corruption laws due to competitive position
- Discussion of how new laws, regulatory changes, and differing standards across markets increase compliance costs and capital expenditures
- Impact of environmental, social, and governance (ESG) regulations and expanded disclosure requirements
- Risks associated with entry into new markets (e.g., alcoholic beverage distribution) requiring additional regulatory compliance

## Litigation and Legal Proceedings
- Company faces various litigation, claims, and regulatory proceedings related to advertising, marketing, product labels, intellectual property, privacy, employment, and environmental matters
- Uncertainty regarding outcomes and potential financial impact of legal matters
- Significant costs and resources required to respond to litigation, even non-meritorious claims
- Potential reputational damage from legal proceedings

## SEC Compliance Status
- No unresolved written comments from SEC staff regarding periodic or current reports

## Corporate Properties and Facilities
- Principal executive offices in Purchase, New York and Plano, Texas (owned)
- Detailed table of significant properties by division, including manufacturing plants, research facilities, concentrate plants, and distribution centers across multiple countries
- Mix of owned and leased properties globally (locations include Iowa, Texas, Mexico, United Kingdom, Russia, Israel, Saudi Arabia, China, Ireland, Singapore, and India)
- Properties generally in good operating condition and adequate for current operations

## Legal Proceedings Summary
- Management believes pending litigation will not have material adverse effect on financial condition or operations

structure.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document from PepsiCo's annual report covers two main sections:

## Item 5: Market for Registrant's Common Equity and Stockholder Matters
- **Stock Information**: PepsiCo's common stock trades under ticker symbol PEP on the Nasdaq Global Select Market and SIX Swiss Exchange
- **Shareholder Base**: Approximately 98,573 shareholders of record as of February 2, 2023
- **Dividend History and Policy**: Consecutive quarterly cash dividends paid since 1965, with declaration discretion held by the Board. Announced a 10% increase in annualized dividend to $5.06 per share (from $4.60)
- **Capital Return Plans**: Expected to return approximately $7.7 billion to shareholders in 2023 ($6.7 billion in dividends and $1.0 billion in share repurchases)
- **Share Repurchase Activity**: Details of 1.8 million shares repurchased during Q4 2022 at an average price of $174.04 per share under a $10 billion repurchase program authorized in February 2022

## Item 7: Management's Discussion and Analysis (Table of Contents)
Outlines the structure of the MD&A section, including:
- Business overview and operations
- Financial results analysis (consolidated and by division: FLNA, QFNA, PBNA, LatAm, Europe, AMESA, APAC)
- Liquidity and capital resources
- Critical accounting policies
- Consolidated financial statements and notes
- Independent auditor's report

structure.title: Management's Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations
structure.summary:
# Description of Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

This partial document is PepsiCo's Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) of Financial Condition and Results of Operations from their Form 10-K filing. The main points covered include:

## Business Overview
- PepsiCo is a leading global beverage and convenient food company with brands like Lay's, Doritos, Gatorade, and Pepsi-Cola, operating in over 200 countries
- The company faced challenges in 2022 including supply chain disruptions, inflationary pressures, COVID-19 impacts, and the Russia-Ukraine conflict
- The company's strategic approach "pep+" focuses on sustainability and human capital through three pillars: Positive Agriculture, Positive Value Chain, and Positive Choices

## Financial Results
- Net revenue grew 9% to $86.4 billion in 2022
- Operating profit increased 3% to $11.5 billion, though operating margin declined 0.7 percentage points
- Net income attributable to PepsiCo was $8.9 billion, up 17% from prior year
- Diluted earnings per share increased 17% to $6.42

## Key Business Risks
- Commodity price volatility and supply chain disruptions
- Climate change regulations and sustainability requirements
- International operations risks and geopolitical uncertainties
- Russia-Ukraine conflict impacts on operations and assets
- Product taxation and regulatory changes
- Retail landscape disruption from e-commerce growth

## Divisional Performance
- FLNA (Frito-Lay North America): 19% revenue growth
- QFNA (Quaker Foods): 15% revenue growth
- PBNA (PepsiCo Beverages North America): 4% revenue growth, significantly impacted by the Juice Transaction
- International divisions (LatAm, Europe, AMESA, APAC) showed varied performance with foreign exchange headwinds

## Major Transactions
- Juice Transaction: Sold Tropicana, Naked, and other juice brands to PAI Partners for $3.5 billion, retaining 39% noncontrolling interest
- Investment in Celsius Holdings for $550 million in convertible preferred stock

## Liquidity and Capital Resources
- Operating cash flow of $10.8 billion in 2022
- Free cash flow of $5.9 billion
- Capital spending of $5.2 billion
- Returned approximately $7.7 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases
- Maintained strong credit ratings and access to capital markets

## Critical Accounting Policies
- Revenue recognition based on product shipment/delivery
- Goodwill and intangible asset impairment testing
- Income tax accounting and uncertain tax positions
- Pension and retiree medical plan assumptions and valuations

## Impairment Charges
- $3.2 billion in total impairment charges in 2022, primarily related to:
  - SodaStream brand in Europe ($1.3 billion)
  - Russia-Ukraine conflict impacts ($1.4 billion)
  - Brand portfolio repositioning and discontinuations ($0.6 billion)

## Risk Management Framework
- Board oversight of enterprise risk management
- PepsiCo Risk Committee for identifying and addressing top risks
- Focus on cybersecurity, food safety, sustainability, and supply chain risks
- Exposure to commodity prices, foreign exchange, and interest rate risks

nodes.title: OUR BUSINESS
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document is from PepsiCo's Form 10-K filing and covers the following main points:

## Business Overview
- PepsiCo is a global beverage and convenient food company with major brands including Lay's, Doritos, Gatorade, Pepsi-Cola, and Mountain Dew, operating in over 200 countries
- The company faced significant challenges in 2022 including supply chain disruptions, inflation, COVID-19, climate concerns, and the Ukraine conflict

## Strategic Initiative: pep+ (PepsiCo Positive)
- A comprehensive sustainability and transformation strategy centered on three pillars:
  - **Positive Agriculture**: Regenerative farming practices across 7 million acres with partnerships including ADM and MIT
  - **Positive Value Chain**: Net-zero emissions by 2040, net water positive by 2030, sustainable packaging, and diversity/inclusion initiatives
  - **Positive Choices**: Portfolio evolution toward healthier products with reduced sugars/sodium and expansion of plant-based options

## Business Risks
- Commodity and supply chain risks from inflation, transportation costs, and labor shortages
- Climate change regulatory impacts
- International operations challenges in emerging markets
- Ukraine conflict effects on operations and assets
- Product taxation and regulatory pressures across jurisdictions
- Retail landscape disruption from e-commerce growth

## Risk Management Framework
- Board oversight of integrated risk management with dedicated committees
- PepsiCo Risk Committee (PRC) for identifying and addressing top risks
- Division-level risk committees and Risk Management Office
- Regular monitoring of cybersecurity, food safety, supply chain, and sustainability risks

## Market Risks and Financial Exposure
- Commodity price derivatives ($1.8 billion notional value)
- Foreign exchange exposure (43% of revenue from international operations)
- Interest rate derivatives ($1.3 billion notional value)
- Discussion of sensitivity analyses for market fluctuations

## Financial Metrics and Volume Measures
- Introduction of key performance metrics including unit volume growth, concentrate shipments and equivalents (CSE), and bottler case sales (BCS)
- Explanation of beverage and convenient food volume measurement methodologies

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document is from PepsiCo's Form 10-K filing and covers the following main points:

## Overview and Purpose
- Management's Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) section intended to help readers understand financial results and condition for 2022 and 2021
- References consolidated financial statements and includes per-share metrics based on common stock

## Business Description
- PepsiCo is a global beverage and convenient food company with major brands (Lay's, Doritos, Cheetos, Gatorade, Pepsi-Cola, Mountain Dew, Quaker, SodaStream)
- Operates in over 200 countries and territories through direct operations, authorized bottlers, and third-party manufacturers

## Key Business Challenges in 2022
- Supply chain disruptions
- Inflationary pressures
- Shifting consumer preferences
- COVID-19 pandemic impacts
- Climate crisis
- Competitive environment and e-commerce growth
- Macroeconomic and political volatility (including Ukraine conflict)
- Evolving regulatory landscape

## Strategic Initiative: pep+ (PepsiCo Positive)
A comprehensive sustainability and transformation strategy with three pillars:
1. **Positive Agriculture**: Regenerative practices across 7 million acres, sustainable sourcing, and supply chain improvements
2. **Positive Value Chain**: Net-zero emissions by 2040, net water positive by 2030, sustainable packaging, and diversity/inclusion initiatives
3. **Positive Choices**: Healthier product portfolio with reduced sugars/sodium, plant-based proteins, and sustainable business models like SodaStream

## Additional Information
- Board member relationships with vendors and customers
- Business risks related to commodities and supply chain volatility

nodes.title: Our Operations
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt outlines a company's (PepsiCo's) sustainability and business strategy, organized into the following main points:

## Strategic Priorities

**Positive Value Chain:** The company is building a circular and inclusive value chain through:
- Achieving net-zero emissions by 2040 and net water positivity by 2030
- Reducing virgin plastic per serving and increasing recycled content in packaging
- Scaling SodaStream globally to eliminate over 200 billion plastic bottles by 2030
- Doubling reusable beverage servings from 10% to 20% by 2030
- Advancing diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives
- Supporting employee community engagement through a global volunteering program

**Positive Choices:** Evolving the product portfolio to be more sustainable through:
- Incorporating diverse, healthier ingredients (chickpeas, plant-based proteins, whole grains)
- Expanding nuts and seeds offerings
- Reducing added sugars and sodium using science-based targets
- Offering products with healthier oils
- Scaling packaging-free business models like SodaStream

## Operational and Risk Information

The document references additional details about business divisions, distribution networks, brands, and intellectual property. It also addresses:
- Board member relationships with vendors and customers
- Business risks related to commodities and supply chain disruptions
- Rising operating costs in transportation, labor, and commodities
- External factors affecting the business (Ukraine conflict, COVID-19, inflation, weather conditions)

nodes.title: Our Business Risks
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Strategic Priorities
- **Positive Value Chain**: Building a circular and inclusive value chain through net-zero emissions by 2040, net water positivity by 2030, sustainable packaging initiatives, reducing virgin plastic, scaling SodaStream to eliminate 200+ billion plastic bottles by 2030, and doubling reusable beverage servings from 10% to 20% by 2030
- **Positive Choices**: Evolving product portfolio with diverse ingredients (chickpeas, plant-based proteins, whole grains), expanding nuts & seeds category, reducing added sugars and sodium, offering healthier oils, and scaling low/no single-use packaging business models

## Business Risks
- **Commodity and Supply Chain Risks**: Higher operating costs in transportation, labor, and commodities; external factors including Ukraine conflict, COVID-19, inflation, weather, and supply chain disruptions
- **Climate Change Risks**: Regulatory requirements for greenhouse gas emissions reduction and carbon pricing programs
- **International Operations Risks**: Unstable economic, political, and social conditions in developing and emerging markets
- **Ukraine Conflict Risks**: Suspended operations in Ukraine, suspended sales of Pepsi-Cola in Russia, geopolitical uncertainty, and significant financial impacts
- **Product Taxation and Regulation**: Various jurisdictions imposing taxes and regulations on beverages, packaging, and ingredients
- **Retail Landscape Disruption**: Growth of e-commerce, mobile commerce, and hard discounters requiring digital capability expansion

## Risk Management Framework
- Board oversight of integrated risk management with dedicated committees
- PepsiCo Risk Committee (PRC) for identifying and addressing top risks
- Division and market-specific risk committees
- Risk Management Office, Internal Audit, and Compliance & Ethics departments
- Disclosure Committee monitoring integrity of disclosure controls

## Market Risks and Financial Exposure
- Commodity price fluctuations managed through derivatives and hedging strategies
- Foreign exchange risks from international operations (43% of net revenue)
- Interest rate exposure managed through derivatives
- Detailed sensitivity analyses for commodity, currency, and interest rate changes

## Financial Metrics and Operations
- Volume metrics (unit volume, concentrate shipments and equivalents, bottler case sales) as key performance indicators
- Discussion of beverage and convenient food volume measurement methodologies
- Impact of 53rd reporting week in fiscal 2022

# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Strategic Priorities
- **Positive Value Chain**: Building a circular and inclusive value chain with goals to achieve net-zero emissions by 2040, become net water positive by 2030, and introduce sustainable packaging solutions including reducing virgin plastic, increasing recycled content, and scaling SodaStream to eliminate over 200 billion plastic bottles by 2030. Also includes diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives and employee volunteering programs.

- **Positive Choices**: Evolving product portfolios to be better for the planet and people through diverse ingredients (chickpeas, plant-based proteins, whole grains), expanding nuts & seeds offerings, reducing added sugars and sodium, offering healthier oils, and scaling reusable packaging business models like SodaStream.

## Business Operations
- Overview of company divisions, distribution networks, brands, intellectual property, and human capital
- Board member relationships with vendors and customers managed to avoid conflicts of interest

## Key Business Risks
- **Commodity and Supply Chain Risks**: Significantly higher operating costs in transportation, labor, and commodities (including energy) expected to continue; exposure to price fluctuations managed through contracts and derivative instruments; external factors like the Ukraine conflict, COVID-19, inflation, weather, and supply chain disruptions impacting availability and costs

- **Climate Change Risks**: Potential increased regulatory requirements for greenhouse gas emissions and carbon pricing programs could result in significant increased costs and capital investments

- **International Operations Risks**: Exposure to unstable economic, political, and social conditions in developing and emerging markets; risks of impairments and write-offs

- **Ukraine Conflict Risks**: Suspended operations in Ukraine, suspended sales of major brands in Russia, negative financial impact; Russia represents 5% of net revenue and 4% of consolidated assets

nodes.title: Risks Associated with Climate Change
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

This document section discusses key business risks and challenges facing the company:

1. **Supply Chain and Cost Pressures**: Raw material and labor shortages have impacted transportation, labor, and commodity availability and costs. The company may face challenges passing price increases to customers without experiencing reduced volume and margins.

2. **Climate Change Risks**: Multiple jurisdictions are implementing or considering new environmental regulations, including greenhouse gas emissions controls and carbon pricing programs. These could result in significant increased costs and capital investments, though the company cannot predict the full scope and timing of future regulations.

3. **International Operations Risks**: The company faces inherent risks from operating in international markets, particularly in developing and emerging markets experiencing unstable economic, political, and social conditions, civil unrest, natural disasters, and currency fluctuations. The company monitors these environments for potential impairments and write-offs.

4. **Ukraine Conflict Impact**: The deadly conflict in Ukraine has created geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainty. The company has suspended operations in Ukraine and suspended sales of major beverage brands in Russia, along with discretionary investments and promotional activities there. Russia represented 5% of net revenue and 4% of consolidated assets as of December 31, 2022, while Ukraine represented 0.2% of net revenue and 0.1% of consolidated assets.

nodes.title: Risks Associated with the Deadly Conflict in Ukraine
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

This document excerpt discusses key business risks and challenges facing the company:

1. **Supply Chain and Commodity Price Risks** - Raw material and labor shortages affecting transportation, availability, and costs, with uncertainty about passing price increases to customers without impacting volume and margins.

2. **Climate Change Regulations** - New or increased environmental requirements in various jurisdictions regarding greenhouse gas emissions and carbon pricing, potentially resulting in significant increased costs and capital investments.

3. **International Operations Risks** - Exposure to unstable economic, political, and social conditions in developing and emerging markets, including currency fluctuations, civil unrest, and potential asset impairments.

4. **Ukraine Conflict Impact** - Significant risks from the ongoing conflict including suspended operations in Ukraine, reduced sales in Russia (5% of net revenue), supply chain disruptions, commodity volatility, cyber risks, and potential asset losses or impairments.

5. **Product and Packaging Taxes/Regulations** - Various jurisdictions imposing new taxes and regulations on beverages, ingredients, packaging materials, and sustainability requirements, with varying impacts across different markets and competitors.

6. **Retail Landscape Disruption** - Industry transformation driven by e-commerce growth, mobile commerce, subscription services, integration of physical and digital retail operations, and expansion of hard discount retailers.

The document emphasizes the company's inability to predict the full scope and timing of these risks and their ultimate financial impact, while noting ongoing monitoring and mitigation efforts.

nodes.title: Imposition of Taxes and Regulations on our Products
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document covers three main risk areas and business challenges:

1. **Russia-Ukraine Conflict Impact**: Details the extensive consequences of the ongoing conflict on business operations, including volatile commodity markets, supply chain disruptions, cyber risks, employee safety concerns, labor shortages, currency volatility, restricted access to credit and banking services, increased transportation and energy costs, asset losses, and impairment charges. The document notes uncertainty about the duration and extent of future impacts on employees, customers, operations, and sustainability goals.

2. **Taxes and Regulations on Products**: Discusses various jurisdictions imposing new or increased taxes and regulations on beverage and food products, their packaging, ingredients, and manufacturing processes. Examples include taxes on caloric beverages and regulations on plastic packaging sustainability. The document emphasizes the unpredictability of these regulations across 200+ countries and territories where the company operates, and notes that regulatory impacts may differ between competitors.

3. **Retail Landscape Disruption**: Introduces changes in the retail industry, including the growth of e-commerce, mobile commerce applications, subscription services, integration of physical and digital retail operations, and expansion of hard discount retailers. The document indicates expectations for continued shifts toward e-commerce and online-to-offline retail models.

2. **Product Taxation and Regulations**: Discusses various jurisdictions imposing new or increased taxes and regulations on beverage and food products, their packaging, and ingredients. Notes the unpredictability of these measures across different regions and their potential differential impact on competitors.

3. **Retail Landscape Disruption**: Addresses the ongoing transformation of retail through e-commerce growth, mobile commerce, subscription services, integration of physical and digital operations, and expansion of hard discounters, with emphasis on the company's need to develop digital capabilities and adapt distribution channels.

4. **Risk Management Framework**: Outlines PepsiCo's integrated approach to identifying, assessing, and managing enterprise risks, including:
   - Board oversight responsibilities for risk management
   - Regular updates on key risks (cybersecurity, food safety, sustainability, human capital, supply chain)
   - Consideration of short-, medium-, and long-term impacts
   - Committee-level oversight of specific risk categories
   - Engagement with external experts on macroeconomic trends

# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers PepsiCo's risk management framework and market risks:

## Main Points:

**Risk Management Overview:**
- Discussion of e-commerce and digital capabilities expansion in response to retail landscape changes and COVID-19 pandemic impacts
- Reference to comprehensive risk management strategies detailed in Risk Factors section

**Risk Management Framework Structure:**
- Board oversight responsibility for integrated risk management across the company
- Board committees with specific risk oversight responsibilities:
  - Audit Committee: Financial, compliance, and employee safety risks
  - Compensation Committee: Employee compensation risk assessment
  - Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee: Governance and succession planning
  - Sustainability, Diversity and Public Policy Committee: Sustainability, diversity, and public policy risks
- PepsiCo Risk Committee (PRC): Cross-functional senior management group identifying and prioritizing top strategic, financial, operating, compliance, and reputational risks
- Division and market-level risk committees for localized risk management
- Risk Management Office providing guidance and analytical support
- Internal Audit Department evaluating internal controls effectiveness
- Compliance & Ethics and Law Departments coordinating compliance policies
- Disclosure Committee monitoring disclosure controls and procedures

**Market Risks:**
- Exposure to commodity price fluctuations affecting raw materials and energy costs
- Foreign exchange rate risks and currency restrictions
- Interest rate risks
- Management strategies including productivity initiatives, global purchasing programs, and hedging
- Detailed analysis of commodity derivatives ($1.8 billion notional value in 2022)
- Foreign exchange exposure with 43% of 2022 revenue from international operations
- Discussion of geopolitical risks in key markets (Argentina, Brazil, China, Mexico, Russia, Turkey, Ukraine)

nodes.title: Market Risks
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the company's exposure to and management of market risks, with detailed discussion of three primary risk categories:

## Market Risk Overview
- The company faces risks from commodity prices, foreign exchange rates/currency restrictions, and interest rates
- Risk management strategies include productivity initiatives, global purchasing programs, and hedging activities
- Fair value of derivatives fluctuates with market rates; indefinite-lived intangible assets are impacted by interest rates and economic conditions

## Commodity Price Risk
- Commodity derivatives totaled $1.8 billion in notional value as of December 31, 2022
- A hypothetical 10% decrease in commodity prices would have reduced net unrealized gains by $176 million, offset by lower commodity purchase costs

## Foreign Exchange Risk
- International operations generated 43% of consolidated net revenue in 2022
- Key markets include Mexico, Russia, Canada, China, UK, and South Africa (23% of revenue)
- Unfavorable foreign exchange reduced net revenue growth by 3 percentage points in 2022
- Foreign currency derivatives totaled $3.0 billion in notional value
- A 10% unfavorable exchange rate change would have decreased net unrealized gains by $298 million
- Debt instruments designated as net investment hedges totaled $2.9 billion

## Interest Rate Risk
- Interest rate derivatives totaled $1.3 billion in notional value
- A 1-percentage-point interest rate increase would have decreased net interest expense by $48 million

## Financial Results and Volume Metrics
- Discussion of unit volume as a key performance metric
- Explanation of beverage volume measurement (concentrate shipments and equivalents vs. bottler case sales)
- Overview of convenient food volume measurement

# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt from PepsiCo's financial report covers several key areas:

## Foreign Currency and Interest Rate Derivatives
- Foreign currency derivatives totaled $3.0 billion notional value (2022) and $2.8 billion (2021)
- Interest rate derivatives totaled $1.3 billion notional value (2022) and $2.1 billion (2021)

## Financial Results Overview
- Consolidated net revenue grew 9% to $86.4 billion in 2022
- Operating profit increased 3% to $11.5 billion, though operating margin declined 0.7 percentage points
- Net income attributable to PepsiCo grew 17% to $8.9 billion
- Diluted earnings per share increased 17% to $6.42

## Division Performance
Detailed results for six divisions (FLNA, QFNA, PBNA, LatAm, Europe, AMESA, APAC) showing:
- Net revenue growth rates ranging from -2% to 21%

- Russia-Ukraine conflict charges and impairments
- Brand portfolio impairment charges
- 2019 Multi-Year Productivity Plan expansion through 2028

## Non-GAAP Measures and Adjustments
Explanation of adjusted financial metrics excluding items affecting comparability, including mark-to-market impacts, restructuring charges, acquisition/divestiture costs, and impairment charges.

Overview of cash sources including operating cash flow, debt financing, and the Juice Transaction proceeds to fund operations, investments, debt repayment, dividends, and share repurchases.

nodes.title: Consolidated Net Revenue and Operating Profit
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section presents PepsiCo's consolidated financial performance for 2022 compared to 2021, covering the following main points:

## Financial Performance Overview
- Net revenue increased 9% to $86.4 billion
- Operating profit grew 3% to $11.5 billion
- Operating margin declined 0.7 percentage points to 13.3%

## Key Drivers of Operating Profit
- Positive factors: net revenue growth and productivity savings
- Negative factors: higher commodity costs (42-point impact), operating cost increases, and impairment charges
- Impact of Juice Transaction: reduced operating profit growth by 3 percentage points but contributed 29 percentage points from the gain on the sale
- Russia-Ukraine conflict charges: 12-percentage-point unfavorable impact
- Brand portfolio impairment charges: 6-percentage-point unfavorable impact
- 53rd reporting week: 1-percentage-point contribution

## Juice Transaction Details
- Sale of Tropicana, Naked, and select juice brands to PAI Partners in Q1 2022
- PepsiCo retained 39% noncontrolling interest in the resulting entity (TBG)
- Divested brands generated approximately $3 billion in 2021 net revenue
- PepsiCo serves as exclusive distributor for TBG in the United States

## Other Consolidated Results
- Pension and retiree medical benefits income decreased $390 million
- Net interest expense decreased $924 million
- Tax rate improved to 16.1% from 21.8%
- Net income increased 17% to $8.9 billion
- Diluted earnings per share increased 17% to $6.42

nodes.title: PBNA
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document presents financial performance results for three geographic business segments:

## PBNA (PepsiCo North America)
- Net revenue grew 4% driven by effective pricing and organic volume growth, partially offset by the Juice Transaction impact
- Unit volume showed mixed results: NCB volume increased 1% (led by Gatorade), while CSD volume declined 1%
- Operating profit surged 122%, primarily due to a $3.0 billion gain from the Juice Transaction, though offset by higher commodity costs (aluminum and resin), transportation, and IT expenses
- The company terminated its Bang energy drink distribution agreement, resulting in brand portfolio impairment charges

## LatAm (Latin America)
- Net revenue increased 21% driven by effective pricing and organic volume growth
- Convenient foods volume grew 3.5%, with strong performance in Mexico offset by Brazil's decline
- Beverage volume grew 6%, led by double-digit growth in Argentina and mid-single-digit growth across multiple countries
- Operating profit increased 19%, supported by revenue growth and productivity savings, but pressured by higher commodity costs (cooking oil, packaging, grains) and increased marketing expenses

## Europe
- Net revenue declined 2% due to unfavorable foreign exchange (9-point impact) and organic volume declines, partially offset by effective pricing
- Convenient foods volume declined 4%, with significant drops in Russia, Ukraine, and Poland
- Beverage volume declined 7%, driven by double-digit declines in Russia, Ukraine, and Germany

nodes.title: Europe
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document presents a comprehensive financial performance analysis of PepsiCo's regional business segments for a reporting period. The main points covered include:

## Regional Performance Overview:
- **PBNA (North America)**: Net revenue grew 4% driven by pricing and organic volume, with operating profit surging 122% largely due to a $3.0 billion gain from the Juice Transaction, offset by higher commodity costs and energy drink portfolio impairment charges.

- **LatAm (Latin America)**: Net revenue increased 21% with strong organic growth across beverages and convenient foods, though operating profit growth of 19% was tempered by significant commodity cost increases and brand portfolio impairment charges.

- **Europe**: Net revenue declined 2% due to unfavorable foreign exchange and the Juice Transaction impact, while operating profit decreased 207% primarily from Russia-Ukraine conflict charges, SodaStream brand impairment, and elevated commodity costs.

- **AMESA (Africa, Middle East, South Asia)**: Net revenue grew 6% from pricing and organic volume, but operating profit decreased 22% due to brand portfolio impairment charges and substantial commodity cost increases.

- **APAC (Asia-Pacific)**: Net revenue increased 4% from pricing and organic growth, with operating profit declining 20% primarily due to Be & Cheery brand impairment charges.

## Key Themes Across Segments:
- Effective net pricing strategies driving revenue growth
- Significant commodity cost pressures (aluminum, resin, packaging materials, cooking oil)
- Multiple brand portfolio impairment and restructuring charges
- Foreign exchange headwinds in several regions
- Volume growth in beverages offsetting convenient foods challenges in some markets

- Russia-Ukraine conflict charges (110 percentage points)

nodes.title: APAC
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Operating Performance Analysis by Region

The document provides detailed financial performance analysis for three geographic segments:

1. **EMEA (Europe, Middle East, Africa)**: Operating profit decreased 207% due to multiple negative factors including Russia-Ukraine conflict charges (110 percentage points), SodaStream brand impairment (98 percentage points), and brand portfolio impairment charges (20 percentage points). These were partially offset by gains from the Juice Transaction. Additional headwinds included higher commodity costs (91 percentage points) and unfavorable foreign exchange (7 percentage points).

2. **AMESA (Africa, Middle East, South Asia)**: Net revenue increased 6% driven by effective net pricing and organic volume growth, though offset by unfavorable foreign exchange (12 percentage points). Operating profit decreased 22% primarily due to brand portfolio impairment charges (19 percentage points), higher commodity costs (74 percentage points), and unfavorable foreign exchange (9 percentage points).

3. **APAC (Asia-Pacific)**: Net revenue increased 4% from effective net pricing and organic volume growth, partially offset by unfavorable foreign exchange (5 percentage points). Operating profit decreased 20% mainly due to Be & Cheery brand impairment charges (25 percentage points) and higher commodity costs (25 percentage points).

## Non-GAAP Financial Measures

The document explains the company's use of non-GAAP financial measures for internal decision-making and investor transparency. It details the types of adjustments made (impairments, restructuring charges, mark-to-market gains/losses, pension-related items) and clarifies that these measures are supplemental to GAAP reporting and should not be used in isolation.

nodes.title: OUR CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES AND ESTIMATES
nodes.summary:
# Management's Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations

## Main Points Covered:

**Business Overview and Strategy**
- PepsiCo is a global beverage and convenient food company with brands including Lay's, Doritos, Gatorade, Pepsi-Cola, and Quaker
- Operating in over 200 countries and territories
- Strategic transformation initiative called "pep+" focused on sustainability and human capital, organized around three pillars: Positive Agriculture, Positive Value Chain, and Positive Choices

**Business Risks and Challenges**
- Supply chain disruptions and inflationary pressures on commodities, transportation, and labor
- Climate change regulations and sustainability requirements
- International operations risks including currency fluctuations and geopolitical instability
- Russia-Ukraine conflict impact on operations and financial results
- Retail landscape disruption from e-commerce growth
- Product taxation and regulatory changes across jurisdictions
- Risk management framework with Board oversight and integrated governance structure

**Financial Performance (2022 vs 2021)**
- Net revenue increased 9% to $86.4 billion
- Operating profit grew 3% to $11.5 billion
- Operating margin declined 0.7 percentage points to 13.3%
- Net income attributable to PepsiCo increased 17% to $8.9 billion
- Diluted earnings per share grew 17% to $6.42

**Division Performance**
- FLNA: 19% revenue growth, 9% operating profit growth
- QFNA: 15% revenue growth, 4.5% operating profit growth
- PBNA: 4% revenue growth, 122% operating profit growth (driven by Juice Transaction gain)
- LatAm: 21% revenue growth, 19% operating profit growth
- Europe: 2% revenue decline, 207% operating profit decline (impacted by Russia-Ukraine conflict)
- AMESA: 6% revenue growth, 22% operating profit decline
- APAC: 4% revenue growth, 20% operating profit decline

**Key Transactions**
- Juice Transaction: Sold Tropicana, Naked and select juice brands to PAI Partners in Q1 2022, retaining 39% noncontrolling interest; generated $3.0 billion gain
- Investment in Celsius Holdings convertible preferred stock and distribution agreement

**Liquidity and Capital Resources**
- Operating cash flow: $10.8 billion (down 7% from prior year)
- Free cash flow: $5.9 billion (down 18% from prior year)
- Capital spending: $5.2 billion
- Returned $7.7 billion to shareholders through dividends ($6.2 billion) and share repurchases ($1.5 billion)
- Announced 10% dividend increase to $5.06 per share effective June 2023

**Critical Accounting Policies**
- Revenue recognition based on product shipment/delivery
- Goodwill and indefinite-lived intangible asset impairment assessments
- Income tax expense and accrual management
- Pension and retiree medical plan assumptions and valuations

**Non-GAAP Measures**
- Organic revenue growth: 14% (excluding FX, acquisitions/divestitures, and 53rd week)
- Core operating profit adjusted for items affecting comparability
- Return on Invested Capital (ROIC): 17.0% (up from 16.0%)
- Core Net ROIC excluding items affecting comparability: 19.6% (up from 18.4%)

**Items Affecting Comparability**
- Restructuring and impairment charges: $380 million
- Acquisition and divestiture-related charges: $74 million
- Impairment and other charges: $3.6 billion (including Russia-Ukraine conflict charges, brand portfolio impairments, and other impairment charges)
- Tax benefit from IRS audit settlement: $319 million

nodes.title: Revenue Recognition
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document outlines the critical accounting policies and estimates for a beverage and convenient food products company. The main points covered include:

## Revenue Recognition
- Revenue is recognized upon shipment or delivery of products when control transfers to customers
- The company maintains reserves for damaged and out-of-date products based on estimates
- Credit terms typically allow 30 days for U.S. payments and 30-90 days internationally
- Expected credit losses are estimated based on past due accounts, collectibility history, and forward-looking information
- Sales incentives and discounts are recorded as revenue reductions
- Accruals for incentive programs (such as bottler funding and volume rebates) are established during the year based on contract terms and historical experience

## Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets
- Brand development costs are expensed as incurred; acquired brands are valued at fair value in business combinations
- Brands with strong revenue performance and ongoing marketplace support are classified as indefinite-lived assets
- Other brands are amortized over 20-40 years based on management judgment
- Certain perpetual franchise rights are considered indefinite-lived intangible assets
- Indefinite-lived intangible assets and goodwill are assessed for impairment at least annually using qualitative or quantitative approaches
- Impairment assessments consider macroeconomic conditions, competitive factors, and regulatory environment
- Fair value determinations require significant estimates including future cash flows, growth rates, and discount rates

nodes.title: Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets

This document section covers accounting policies and practices for a large beverage company, focusing on three main areas:

## Goodwill and Other Intangible Assets

- Details the criteria for determining whether brands have indefinite or finite useful lives (20-40 years), based on revenue performance, market share, and company support
- Describes franchise rights treatment, including indefinite-lived rights for exclusive beverage distribution territories
- Outlines the annual impairment assessment process for indefinite-lived intangible assets and goodwill, using both qualitative and quantitative approaches
- Notes a $1.3 billion impairment charge recorded in 2022 for the SodaStream brand in Europe and discusses sensitivity to future changes in discount rates and growth assumptions
- Explains impairment testing for amortizable intangible assets upon significant operational or macroeconomic changes

## Income Tax Expense and Accruals
- Describes the determination of annual effective tax rates based on income, statutory rates, and tax structure across jurisdictions
- Explains the establishment and adjustment of tax reserves for positions subject to challenge
- Details the application of estimated quarterly tax rates and separate calculation of taxes on significant items
- Clarifies the differences between book income (consolidated financial statements) and taxable income (tax returns), including permanent and temporary differences
- Explains the creation of deferred tax assets and liabilities and the use of valuation allowances

# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt covers key financial and operational information from PepsiCo's annual report, focusing on three main areas:

## 1. Pension and Retiree Medical Plans
- Details the company's pension plans covering U.S. and international employees, with benefits based on years of service and/or earnings
- Describes retiree medical benefits for eligible U.S. and Canadian retirees, with company costs capped at specified amounts
- Explains the four components of pension and retiree medical expenses: service cost, interest cost, gains/losses, and expected return on assets
- Lists significant actuarial assumptions including demographic factors, asset return rates, discount rates, salary increases, and healthcare cost trends
- Provides weighted-average assumptions for 2023, 2022, and 2021, showing increases in discount rates year-over-year
- Discusses sensitivity analysis indicating that a 25-basis-point decrease in discount rates would increase 2023 expense by $13 million, while a similar decrease in expected return rates would increase expense by $38 million
- Details funding strategy, including discretionary contributions of $125 million made in January 2023 with an additional $125 million expected in Q3 2023

## 2. Tax Rate Comparison

## 3. Consolidated Statement of Income

- Includes diluted earnings per share of $6.42 in 2022 (up from $5.49 in 2021)

# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the company's pension and retiree medical plans, including:

## Key Topics:

1. **Plan Overview**: Description of pension plans covering U.S. and international employees, with benefits based on years of service or combined service and earnings. Retiree medical benefits available to eligible U.S. and Canadian retirees with capped company contributions.

2. **Tax Rate Information**: 2022 annual tax rate of 16.1% compared to 21.8% in 2021.

3. **Expense Components**: Four main components of annual pension and retiree medical expenses—service cost, interest cost, gains/losses, and expected return on assets.

4. **Key Assumptions**: Details on demographic factors, expected asset returns, discount rates, salary increase rates, and health care cost trend rates used to calculate expenses and obligations.

5. **Discount Rate Methodology**: Explanation of how discount rates are determined using the Mercer Above Mean Curve based on high-quality corporate debt securities.

6. **Weighted-Average Assumptions Table**: Comparative data for 2023, 2022, and 2021 showing service cost discount rates, interest cost discount rates, expected returns, salary increases, and health care trend rates.

7. **2022 Remeasurement Impact**: Discussion of lump sum distributions triggering settlement charges and expected expense increases in 2023.

8. **Sensitivity Analysis**: Impact of 25-basis-point changes in discount rates and expected return assumptions on 2023 expenses.

9. **Funding Strategy**: Explanation of contribution practices, including discretionary contributions of $125 million made in January 2023 with additional $125 million expected in Q3 2023, and pay-as-you-go funding for retiree medical plans.

nodes.title: Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This partial document contains the Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements from PepsiCo, Inc.'s financial report, covering key accounting policies and financial disclosures.

## Main Points Covered:

**Organizational Structure & Reporting:**
- PepsiCo operates through seven reportable segments: Frito-Lay North America (FLNA), Quaker Foods North America (QFNA), PepsiCo Beverages North America (PBNA), Latin America (LatAm), Europe, Africa/Middle East/South Asia (AMESA), and Asia Pacific/Australia/New Zealand/China (APAC)
- Company serves over 200 countries with major operations in the U.S., Mexico, Russia, Canada, China, UK, and South Africa

**Financial Performance & Charges:**
- 2022 net revenue of $86.4 billion with operating profit of $11.5 billion
- Significant impairment charges totaling $3.6 billion related to Russia-Ukraine conflict, brand portfolio repositioning, and indefinite-lived intangible asset assessments
- Restructuring charges of $411 million under the 2019 Productivity Plan

**Key Accounting Policies:**
- Revenue recognition upon product shipment/delivery
- Goodwill and indefinite-lived intangible assets assessed for impairment annually
- Pension and retiree medical plan obligations and funding strategies
- Share-based compensation programs including stock options, RSUs, and PSUs

**Major Transactions:**
- Juice Transaction: Sale of Tropicana and Naked brands to PAI Partners for $3.5 billion with 39% retained interest in TBG
- 2020 acquisitions: Pioneer Foods, Rockstar energy drinks, and Be & Cheery

**Financial Instruments & Risk Management:**
- Derivative instruments used to manage commodity, foreign exchange, and interest rate risks
- Debt obligations of $38.8 billion with various maturity dates
- Lease obligations and operating lease accounting

**Tax Matters:**
- Effective tax rate of 16.1% in 2022
- Ongoing IRS audits and uncertain tax positions
- Mandatory transition tax liability under Tax Cuts and Jobs Act

# Description of Partial Document

This partial document contains Note 1 and the beginning of Note 2 from PepsiCo's Consolidated Financial Statements, covering the following main points:

## Basis of Presentation
- Consolidated financial statements prepared in accordance with GAAP
- Includes PepsiCo and controlled affiliates; uses equity method for 50% or less ownership interests
- Venezuelan subsidiaries excluded due to operating restrictions
- Details on cost classification (production costs in cost of sales; distribution/merchandising in SG&A)
- Discussion of estimates and assumptions used in financial reporting, noting increased difficulty due to Russia-Ukraine conflict and inflationary environment
- Fiscal year ends last Saturday of December; 2022 included 53-week reporting period

## Organizational Structure - Seven Reportable Segments
- Frito-Lay North America (FLNA)
- Quaker Foods North America (QFNA)
- PepsiCo Beverages North America (PBNA)
- Latin America (LatAm)
- Europe
- Africa, Middle East and South Asia (AMESA)
- Asia Pacific, Australia, New Zealand and China (APAC)

## Division Accounting Policies
- Share-based compensation, pension/retiree medical expense, and derivatives allocation methodologies
- Net revenue and operating profit/loss by division for 2020-2022
- Revenue disaggregation between beverages and convenient foods

## Impairment and Other Charges
- Russia-Ukraine conflict charges ($1,415 million pre-tax)
- Brand portfolio impairment charges ($648 million pre-tax)
- Other impairment charges on indefinite-lived intangible assets ($1,555 million pre-tax)
- COVID-19 related charges by division

## Division Financial Information
- Total assets and capital spending by division
- Amortization of intangible assets and depreciation by division
- Net revenue and long-lived assets by country

## Revenue Recognition Policy (Note 2)
- Revenue recognized upon shipment/delivery when control transfers to customers
- Policy for damaged/out-of-date product removal and replacement

   - Quaker Foods North America (QFNA) - cereals, rice, pasta and branded foods in US and Canada
   - PepsiCo Beverages North America (PBNA) - beverage businesses in US and Canada
   - Latin America (LatAm) - beverages and convenient foods across Latin America
   - Europe - beverages and convenient foods across Europe
   - Africa, Middle East and South Asia (AMESA) - beverages and convenient foods in these regions
   - Asia Pacific, Australia, New Zealand and China (APAC) - beverages and convenient foods in these regions

2. **Global Operations**: The company operates in over 200 countries and territories with major operations in the United States, Mexico, Russia, Canada, China, the United Kingdom, and South Africa.

nodes.title: Share-Based Compensation Expense
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section describes PepsiCo's organizational structure and financial performance across its seven reportable business divisions:

## Organizational Structure
- **Seven Divisions**: FLNA (Frito-Lay North America), QFNA (Quaker Foods North America), PBNA (PepsiCo Beverages North America), LatAm (Latin America), Europe, AMESA (Africa, Middle East and South Asia), and APAC (Asia Pacific, Australia, New Zealand and China)
- Operations span over 200 countries with major presence in the United States, Mexico, Russia, Canada, China, United Kingdom, and South Africa

## Accounting Allocation Methodologies
The document explains how three key expense categories are allocated to divisions:
- **Share-Based Compensation**: Allocated as incremental employee costs, with PBNA receiving the largest allocation (20% in 2022)
- **Pension and Retiree Medical Expense**: Reflected at fixed discount rates in division results
- **Derivatives**: Centrally managed commodity derivatives (agricultural products, energy, metals) with mark-to-market gains/losses recorded in corporate expenses

## Financial Performance (2020-2022)
Detailed tables showing:
- **Net Revenue**: Total division revenue grew from $70.4 billion (2020) to $86.4 billion (2022)
- **Operating Profit/Loss**: Total division operating profit increased from $11.5 billion (2020) to $13.6 billion (2022)
- Notable items include Europe's operating loss in 2022 and significant gains from the Juice Transaction in PBNA and Europe divisions

# Description of Partial Document

The guidance requires enhanced transparency and disclosure of key terms, payment details, unpaid amounts, balance sheet presentation, and rollforward information. The guidance becomes effective in Q1 2023 (with rollforward requirements effective in 2024), and the company plans to adopt it when effective.

2. **Note 3 — Restructuring and Impairment Charges**: Describes the 2019 Multi-Year Productivity Plan, which was expanded and extended through 2028 in Q4 2022. The plan aims to simplify operations, automate processes, and optimize manufacturing and supply chain. Key points include:
   - Expected total pre-tax charges of approximately $3.65 billion ($2.9 billion in cash expenditures)
   - Charge composition: 55% severance/employee costs, 10% asset impairments, 35% other implementation costs

The guidance is effective in Q1 2023 (with rollforward requirements effective in 2024), and the company plans to adopt it when effective.

2. **Restructuring and Impairment Charges (Note 3)**: Details the 2019 Multi-Year Productivity Plan, which was expanded and extended through 2028. The plan involves leveraging technology and business models to simplify processes, re-engineer systems, and optimize manufacturing and supply chains. Expected total pre-tax charges are approximately $3.65 billion ($2.9 billion in cash expenditures), consisting of 55% severance costs, 10% asset impairments, and 35% other implementation costs. The document provides a breakdown of charges by division, historical charges from 2020-2022, and a liability reconciliation showing cumulative charges of $1.455 billion through December 31, 2022.

3. **Intangible Assets (Note 4)**: Summarizes amortizable intangible assets including acquired franchise rights, customer relationships, brands, and other identifiable intangibles, with gross values, accumulated amortization, and net values for 2022 and 2021. It also includes five-year amortization projections and discusses impairment evaluation procedures, including a $241 million impairment charge related to discontinued juice and dairy brands in Russia.

# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the company's tax matters and deferred tax positions as of December 31, 2022. The main points include:

## Mandatory Transition Tax (TCJ Act)
- Outstanding liability of $2.6 billion payable through 2026
- Reduced liability by $309 million in both 2022 and 2021, with $309 million expected to be paid in 2023
- Company elected to treat Global Intangible Low-Tax Income (GILTI) as current-period expense rather than deferred tax

## IRS Audit and Tax Settlements
- Received final IRS assessment for tax years 2014-2016, resulting in $112 million non-cash tax expense in 2021
- Settled one issue with the IRS in 2022 covering tax years 2014-2019, resulting in $233 million non-cash tax benefit
- Other issues remain under audit for tax years 2014-2019

## Recent Tax Legislation
- Inflation Reduction Act (August 2022) not expected to materially impact financial results
- Swiss tax reform (TRAF) effective January 1, 2020, resulted in $72 million tax benefit in 2020

## Deferred Tax Position
- Net deferred tax liability of $71 million as of December 31, 2022 (compared to $516 million in 2021)
- Gross deferred tax liabilities of $4.337 billion and gross deferred tax assets of $9.421 billion
- Valuation allowances of $5.013 billion applied against deferred tax assets
- Key deferred tax assets include net carryforwards ($5.342 billion) and intangible assets ($1.614 billion)

nodes.title: Stock Options
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers PepsiCo's share-based compensation accounting policies and activity for the year ended December 31, 2022. The main points include:

## Share-Based Compensation Policies
- PepsiCo records compensation expense only for awards expected to vest and does not backdate, reprice, or grant awards retroactively
- Repricing requires shareholder approval under the Long-Term Incentive Plan (LTIP)

## Stock Options
- Employees can purchase PepsiCo common stock at a specified exercise price
- Valued using the Black-Scholes model with a 10-year term
- Key assumptions for 2022 include: 7-year expected life, 1.9% risk-free rate, 16% volatility, and 2.5% dividend yield
- 2022 activity shows 10.5 million options outstanding with weighted-average exercise price of $124.63

## Restricted Stock Units (RSUs) and Performance Stock Units (PSUs)
- RSUs represent obligations to deliver one share upon vesting
- PSUs deliver shares based on PepsiCo's performance against specified financial metrics
- Both accrue dividend equivalents paid in cash upon vesting
- Fair value measured at market price on grant date
- 2022 activity shows 5.7 million RSUs/PSUs outstanding with weighted-average grant-date fair value of $143.02

## Long-Term Cash Awards
- Granted to executive officers and senior executives
- Payout based on Total Shareholder Return relative to peer companies and performance targets over three-year periods
- Valued using Monte Carlo simulation model on a mark-to-market basis

# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers PepsiCo's share-based compensation and pension plans:

## Main Points:

**Restricted Stock Units (RSUs) and Performance Stock Units (PSUs)**
- RSUs represent obligations to deliver one share of PepsiCo common stock upon vesting
- PSUs deliver shares based on achievement of specified financial performance metrics, with share counts adjustable between minimum and maximum thresholds
- Both accrue dividend equivalents paid in cash upon vesting
- Fair value measured at market price on grant date
- 2022 activity shows 5,714 thousand RSUs/PSUs outstanding at year-end with weighted-average grant-date fair value of $143.02

**Long-Term Cash Awards**
- Granted to executive officers and senior executives
- Payout based on PepsiCo's Total Shareholder Return relative to peer companies over three-year performance periods
- Valued using Monte Carlo simulation model on mark-to-market basis
- 2022 outstanding balance of $50.3 million at target, with fair value of $68.2 million

**Other Share-Based Compensation**
- Stock options, RSUs/PSUs activity and metrics for 2020-2022
- Includes phantom stock units under Director Deferral Program (307,000 outstanding as of December 31, 2022)

**Pension and Retiree Plans**

- Transfer of $145 million in pension and retiree medical obligations to TBG in connection with Juice Transaction

- Pension and retiree medical obligations of $145 million were transferred to TBG in connection with the Juice Transaction in 2022

# Description of Partial Document

# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers the company's risk management and hedging strategies across multiple risk categories:

## Main Points:

1. **Hedging Accounting Treatment**: Discusses how hedging gains and losses are recognized in earnings, with 2022 reclassifications noted as immaterial. Cash flows from derivatives are classified as operating activities.

2. **Credit Risk Management**: Details the company's assessment of counterparty credit risk, which is considered low. Describes collateral requirements tied to credit ratings (A2/Moody's or A/S&P) and reports $235 million in derivative instruments in net liability positions as of December 31, 2022, with no collateral posted.

3. **Commodity Price Risk**: Explains management of commodity price exposure through fixed-price contracts, pricing agreements, and derivatives (swaps and futures) with terms up to three years. Covers accounting treatment for derivatives not qualifying for hedge accounting. Reports commodity derivatives notional value of $1.8 billion (2022) and $1.6 billion (2021).

4. **Foreign Exchange Risk**: Describes exposure to foreign exchange risks in international operations and management strategies including local sourcing, local currency contracts, and forward contracts (up to two years). Covers transaction gains/losses recognition and net investment hedges. Reports foreign currency derivatives notional value of $3.0 billion (2022) and $2.8 billion (2021).

nodes.title: Foreign Exchange
nodes.summary:
# Main Points Covered in the Partial Document

## Hedging and Derivative Management
- Recognition of hedging gains/losses in earnings with immaterial reclassifications in 2022
- Classification of derivative cash flows as operating activities
- Consistent treatment of earnings and cash flow impacts with underlying hedged items

## Credit Risk Assessment
- Regular counterparty credit risk assessments deemed low
- Diversification across multiple creditworthy financial institutions
- Collateral requirements triggered at credit ratings below A2 (Moody's) or A (S&P)
- $235 million fair value of derivatives in net liability position with no collateral posted as of December 31, 2022

## Commodity Price Risk Management
- Use of fixed-price contracts, pricing agreements, and derivatives (swaps and futures)
- Hedging of agricultural products, energy, and metals with derivative terms up to three years
- Mark-to-market accounting for non-qualifying hedges
- Total notional value of $1.8 billion in commodity derivatives (2022) vs. $1.6 billion (2021)

## Foreign Exchange Risk Management
- Exposure from international operations and net investments in foreign subsidiaries
- Risk mitigation through local sourcing and forward contracts (up to two years)
- Net investment hedges for foreign subsidiary investments
- Total notional value of $3.0 billion in foreign currency derivatives (2022) vs. $2.8 billion (2021)
- $2.9 billion in debt instruments designated as net investment hedges (2022)

## Interest Rate Risk Management
- Central management of debt and investment portfolios
- Use of interest rate swaps, cross-currency swaps, Treasury locks, and swap locks
- Approximately 1% of total debt subject to variable rates as of December 31, 2022
- Total notional value of $1.3 billion in interest rate derivatives (2022) vs. $2.1 billion (2021)

## Debt Securities Investment
- Held-to-maturity securities recorded at amortized cost
- Available-for-sale securities reported at fair value with changes in accumulated other comprehensive loss
- $550 million investment in Celsius Series A convertible preferred shares with conversion and redemption features

Interest rate derivatives had a notional value of $1.3 billion (2022) and $2.1 billion (2021), with approximately 1% of total debt subject to variable rates as of December 2022.

# Description of Partial Document

This document excerpt covers two main sections from PepsiCo's financial notes:

## Note 13 — Acquisitions and Divestitures

**Juice Transaction (2022):**
- PepsiCo sold Tropicana, Naked, and other juice brands to PAI Partners for approximately $3.5 billion in cash plus a 39% noncontrolling interest in TBG (The Beverage Group)
- Transaction completed in two phases: North America (January 24, 2022) and Europe (February 1, 2022)
- PepsiCo acts as exclusive distributor for TBG in the U.S. and accounts for the investment using the equity method
- Generated a gain of $3.321 billion before taxes in 2022, resulting in net income impact of $2.888 billion ($2.08 per share)
- Included $520 million gain from remeasurement of TBG ownership at fair value
- Assets held for sale totaled $1.8 billion as of December 25, 2021

**2020 Acquisitions:**
- **Pioneer Foods** (South Africa): Acquired March 23, 2020 for approximately $1.2 billion; includes socioeconomic commitments to South Africa Competition Commission valued at approximately $0.5 billion
- **Rockstar** (Energy drinks): Acquired April 24, 2020 for $3.85 billion upfront plus $0.88 billion in contingent consideration
- **Be & Cheery** (China online food): Acquired June 1, 2020 for approximately $0.7 billion

**Acquisition and Divestiture-Related Charges:**
- Summary of charges across 2020-2022 by division and transaction type, including fair value adjustments, merger costs, and integration expenses

## Note 14 — Supplemental Financial Information

Detailed balance sheet items including:
- Accounts and notes receivable with allowance reconciliation
- Inventory breakdown (raw materials, work-in-process, finished goods)
- Property, plant and equipment with useful lives and depreciation
- Other assets and current liabilities with comparative 2022 and 2021 figures

nodes.title: Juice Transaction
nodes.summary:
# Description of Partial Document

This document section covers PepsiCo's acquisitions and divestitures, with primary focus on three main areas:

## Juice Transaction (2022)
- Sale of Tropicana, Naked, and other juice brands to PAI Partners for approximately $3.5 billion in cash plus a 39% noncontrolling interest in TBG
- Transaction completed in two phases: North America (January 24, 2022) and Europe (February 1, 2022)
- PepsiCo retained exclusive distributor rights for TBG's brands in the U.S. for small-format and foodservice customers
- Generated a gain of $3.321 billion, resulting in net income impact of $2.888 billion ($2.08 per share) after taxes
- Included $520 million gain from remeasurement of TBG investment at fair value
- Assets held for sale as of December 25, 2021 totaled $1.8 billion, with liabilities of $0.8 billion
- Transition services agreement established to facilitate orderly business transition

## 2020 Acquisitions
Three major acquisitions completed in 2020:

1. **Pioneer Foods** (March 23, 2020): South African food and beverage company acquired for approximately $1.2 billion; includes commitments to South Africa Competition Commission valued at approximately $0.5 billion for employee benefits, agricultural development, and education

2. **Rockstar** (April 24, 2020): Energy drink maker acquired for $3.85 billion upfront plus $0.88 billion in contingent consideration for estimated future tax benefits

3. **Be & Cheery** (June 1, 2020): Chinese online convenient food company acquired for approximately $0.7 billion

All three acquisitions included detailed fair value allocations of identifiable assets, intangible assets, and resulting goodwill amounts.

nodes.title: 2020 Acquisitions
nodes.summary:
# Description of 2020 Acquisitions and Related Charges

## Main Points Covered:

**2020 Major Acquisitions:**
- **Pioneer Foods** (March 23, 2020): South African food and beverage company acquired for approximately $1.2 billion; includes significant commitments to South Africa Competition Commission totaling ~$0.5 billion for employee benefits, agricultural development, education, and enterprise development programs
- **Rockstar** (April 24, 2020): Energy drink maker acquired for $3.85 billion upfront plus $0.88 billion in contingent consideration related to future tax benefits
- **Be & Cheery** (June 1, 2020): Chinese online convenient food company acquired for approximately $0.7 billion

**Purchase Price Allocation Details:**
- Detailed breakdown of identifiable assets acquired, liabilities assumed, and resulting goodwill for each acquisition
- Total purchase prices: Pioneer Foods ($1.226 billion), Rockstar ($4.729 billion), Be & Cheery ($0.698 billion)
- Goodwill amounts and their strategic rationale for each acquisition

**Goodwill Analysis:**
- Pioneer Foods goodwill ($558 million) reflects expected synergies from combined brand portfolios and distribution networks
- Rockstar goodwill ($2.278 billion) represents expected innovation in energy category
- Be & Cheery goodwill ($309 million) reflects growth opportunities through direct-to-consumer and supply chain capabilities

**Acquisition and Divestiture-Related Charges:**
- Summary of charges across 2020-2022 periods, including fair value adjustments, merger and integration costs, and divestiture expenses
- Impact on net income per common share and after-tax amounts
- Breakdown by division and transaction type

- **Specific Acquisitions**: Details goodwill recorded from three major acquisitions:
  - Pioneer Foods (AMESA division): reflects brand portfolio and distribution network synergies; non-deductible
  - Rockstar (PBNA division): represents expected innovation in energy category; tax-deductible
  - Be & Cheery (APAC division): reflects growth opportunities from direct-to-consumer and supply chain capabilities; non-deductible
- **Acquisition and Divestiture Charges**: Summarizes charges from 2020-2022 including fair value adjustments, merger and integration costs, and divestiture expenses, with detailed breakdowns by division and impact on net income per share

## 2. Supplemental Financial Information (Balance Sheet)

- **Other Assets**: Includes noncurrent receivables, deferred marketplace spending, pension plans, and right-of-use assets
- **Accounts Payable and Current Liabilities**: Breaks down payables, accrued expenses, and other current obligations

This partial document from Note 14 — Supplemental Financial Information presents detailed balance sheet and cash flow data for a company across multiple years (2020-2022). The main points covered include:

- **Inventories**: Total inventory grew from $4.3 billion (2021) to $5.2 billion (2022), with breakdowns by raw materials, work-in-process, and finished goods; increases attributed to higher commodity costs
- **Property, Plant and Equipment**: Net PP&E increased from $22.4 billion (2021) to $24.3 billion (2022), including land, buildings, machinery, and construction in progress, with depreciation expense detailed
- **Other Assets**: Including noncurrent receivables, deferred marketplace spending, pension plans, right-of-use assets, and other investments totaling $5.3 billion (2022)
- **Accounts Payable and Current Liabilities**: Total current liabilities increased from $21.2 billion (2021) to $23.4 billion (2022), including accounts payable, accrued expenses, and lease liabilities

**Cash Flow Information:**
